blocks.content: Davenport came up to the porch. He didn't sit. He stood with his arms at his sides and his hat still on and looked at Julian the way he looked at things he was trying to decide how much weight to give.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: Friday night, around eleven. I was walking the beach. Your group next door had a fire going — six people. I wasn't stopping to watch. I was moving. But I read a room the same way other people breathe. I don't have to try.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: Five of them were present. Watching the fire, watching each other, in the loose easy way people move when they're comfortable with the company. You know the posture — nobody's performing, nobody's tracking the time.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: The sixth had a fixed point. One person in that group, and only that person, for the entire time I was within line of sight. Not glancing. Not the way you look at someone you're fond of. Fixed. The attention was directional and deliberate and it had nothing warm in it.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: You got all of that walking past a bonfire at night.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: I got all of that in about eight seconds. Yes.
Davenport said nothing. Let it sit.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: I'm telling you because I noticed it Friday, spent all of Saturday trying to file it away, and then watched you arrive with the lights going at one in the morning.
